summary:
Among the earliest known paved roads in the world is a limestone-surfaced road in Egypt, dating to approximately 2600–2500 BCE, used to transport large stone blocks from a basalt quarry to the Nile. However, evidence of stone-paved pathways and urban street surfaces appears at Mesopotamian and Indus Valley sites from the fourth millennium BCE onward, including paved drainage systems at Harappan cities such as [[event:1681|Mohenjo-daro]]. In Mesopotamia, urban streets during the [[event:1617|Uruk period]] were often unpaved but well defined by the arrangement of mudbrick buildings.

Early paved roads served primarily to facilitate the movement of goods, particularly by wheeled vehicles and draft animals, and to manage runoff within urban environments. The cognitive and organizational leap represented by deliberately prepared road surfaces — which required planning, resource allocation, and communal labor — is one indicator of the increasing administrative sophistication of early cities. Road infrastructure in the ancient world would eventually become one of the defining tools of imperial expansion and economic integration.
